attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-week history of a mildly itchy, red rash in his beard area. He works as a farmer and regularly handles cattle. His family does not have similar symptoms. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are erythematous, scaly plaques with scattered pustules and broken hair shafts in the beard area. The affected areas are only mildly tender to touch and the hair falls aways easily on touching. He denies any fevers or feeling otherwise unwell.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment for the new symptoms?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of tinea barbae, a dermatophyte infection of facial hair follicles from infected animals. The occupational history of farming is significant as Trichophyton verrucosum from cattle is a common cause. The presence of broken hair shafts, scaling, and red pustules in the beard area are characteristic features. Tinea barbae refers to the previous hypothesis that poor hygiene from shared razors at the barbers caused the infection. It is differentiated from bacterial folliculitis by the lack of significant pain and the hair falls away easily. This patient is also systemically otherwise well with no fever or unwell feelings which can occur with bacterial folliculitis. Kerions can also develop as a complication of tinea barbae.

Oral terbinafine is the most appropriate treatment as topical agents cannot penetrate adequately into hair follicles. A typical course would be 250mg daily for 2-4 weeks. Oral antifungal therapy is necessary for all hair-bearing skin infections to achieve adequate drug levels in the hair follicle.

Topical clotrimazole cream would be inadequate for treating tinea barbae due to poor penetration into hair follicles, although it may be effective for superficial dermatophyte infections without hair involvement.

Topical hydrocortisone cream would be inappropriate as it may worsen fungal infections and mask symptoms without treating the underlying cause. Steroids can lead to a condition called tinea incognito where the typical features are altered.

Oral flucloxacillin would not be effective against dermatophyte fungi. While bacterial folliculitis can present similarly, the presence of scaling and broken hair shafts is more suggestive of a fungal infection.

Oral tretinoin is a retinoid used for acne treatment and would not be effective against fungal infections. It has no antifungal properties and could potentially irritate the affected skin further.
learningPoints: Tinea barbae is a dermatophyte infection of facial hair follicles typically caused by Trichophyton species.
Oral antifungal therapy is required for tinea barbae due to hair follicle involvement.
Clinical features include erythematous plaques with scaling, pustules, and broken hair shafts in the beard area.
references: uruvella T, Pandey S. Tinea Barbae. 2023 Jun 25. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024.  Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33085351/
DermNet NZ. Tinea Barbae. DermNet NZ. 2003.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/tinea-barbae

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
